structuredFacts.title: Photosynthesis
structuredFacts.subtitle: Biological process to convert light into chemical energy
structuredFacts.leadParagraph:
Photosynthesis is a system of biological processes by which photopigment-bearing autotrophic organisms, such as most plants, algae and cyanobacteria, convert light energy — typically from sunlight — into the chemical energy necessary to fuel their metabolism. The term photosynthesis usually refers to oxygenic photosynthesis, a process that releases oxygen as a byproduct of water splitting. Photosynthetic organisms store the converted chemical energy within the bonds of intracellular organic compounds (complex compounds containing carbon), typically carbohydrates like sugars (mainly glucose, fructose and sucrose), starches, phytoglycogen and cellulose. When needing to use this stored energy, an organism's cells then metabolize the organic compounds through cellular respiration. Photosynthesis plays a critical role in producing and maintaining the oxygen content of the Earth's atmosphere, and it supplies most of the biological energy necessary for complex life on Earth.
Some organisms also perform anoxygenic photosynthesis, which does not produce oxygen. Some bacteria (e.g. purple bacteria) use bacteriochlorophyll to split hydrogen sulfide as a reductant instead of water, releasing sulfur instead of oxygen, which was a dominant form of photosynthesis in the euxinic Canfield oceans during the Boring Billion. Archaea such as Halobacterium also perform a type of non-carbon-fixing anoxygenic photosynthesis, where the simpler photopigment retinal and its microbial rhodopsin derivatives are used to absorb green light and produce a proton (hydron) gradient across the cell membrane, and the subsequent ion movement powers transmembrane proton pumps to directly synthesize adenosine triphosphate (ATP), the "energy currency" of cells. Such archaeal photosynthesis might have been the earliest form of photosynthesis that evolved on Earth, as far back as the Paleoarchean, preceding that of cyanobacteria (see Purple Earth hypothesis).
While the details may differ between species, the process always begins when light energy is absorbed by the reaction centers, proteins that contain photosynthetic pigments or chromophores. In plants, these pigments are chlorophylls (a porphyrin derivative that absorbs the red and blue spectra of light, thus reflecting green) held inside chloroplasts, abundant in leaf cells. In cyanobacteria, they are embedded in the plasma membrane. In these light-dependent reactions, some energy is used to strip electrons from suitable substances, such as water, producing oxygen gas. The hydrogen freed by the splitting of water is used in the creation of two important molecules that participate in energetic processes: reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) and ATP.
In plants, algae, and cyanobacteria, sugars are synthesized by a subsequent sequence of light-independent reactions called the Calvin cycle. In this process, atmospheric carbon dioxide is incorporated into already existing organic compounds, such as ribulose bisphosphate (RuBP). Using the ATP and NADPH produced by the light-dependent reactions, the resulting compounds are then reduced and removed to form further carbohydrates, such as glucose. In other bacteria, different mechanisms like the reverse Krebs cycle are used to achieve the same end.
The first photosynthetic organisms probably evolved early in the evolutionary history of life using reducing agents such as hydrogen or hydrogen sulfide, rather than water, as sources of electrons. Cyanobacteria appeared later; the excess oxygen they produced contributed directly to the oxygenation of the Earth, which rendered the evolution of complex life possible. The average rate of energy captured by global photosynthesis is approximately 130 terawatts, which is about eight times the total power consumption of human civilization. Photosynthetic organisms also convert around 100–115 billion tons (91–104 Pg petagrams, or billions of metric tons), of carbon into biomass per year. Photosynthesis was discovered in 1779 by Jan Ingenhousz who showed that plants need light, not just soil and water.
structuredFacts.briefSummary: Jan Ingenhousz discovered photosynthesis in 1779, demonstrating that plants require light—not just soil and water—to fuel the process. Today, we know that photosynthetic organisms convert between 100 and 115 billion metric tons of carbon into biomass annually. This process captures energy at a global rate of approximately 130 terawatts, a capacity about eight times the total power consumption of human civilization. Long before this water-splitting systems evolved, however, early organisms relied on hydrogen sulfide as an electron donor, releasing elemental sulfur instead of oxygen.

cards.title: The Dark Pre-Oxygenic World
cards.summary: Long before water-splitting photosynthesis evolved, early organisms relied on hydrogen sulfide as an electron donor, releasing elemental sulfur instead of oxygen. Because the early Earth lacked free atmospheric oxygen and water-splitting systems, these primitive organisms were forced to depend on alternative electron donors for metabolic energy. Certain microbes even performed photosynthesis by using sunlight to oxidize toxic arsenite into arsenate, yielding carbon monoxide as a byproduct to build other compounds. Through these diverse pathways, microbes successfully generated energy and biomass while releasing elemental sulfur, carbon monoxide, and arsenate into the primordial environment. Other early evolutionary forms of photosynthesis included retinal-based, non-carbon-fixing anoxygenic pathways in archaea like Halobacterium. Ultimately, early life thrived by photosynthesizing toxic compounds in a world completely devoid of oxygen.
cards.readerQuestion: How did early life generate energy before the atmosphere contained oxygen?
cards.keyTakeaway: Early life thrived by photosynthesizing toxic compounds without oxygen.

cards.title: The Dawn of Photosynthesis
cards.summary: Dutch physician Jan Ingenhousz discovered photosynthesis in 1779. At the time, scientists were struggling to determine whether plants relied solely on soil and water for their growth. Through his experiments, Ingenhousz proved that plants actually require light to carry out this vital process. By demonstrating this relationship, he showed that plants need sunlight to generate oxygen, revealing the basic mechanics of photosynthesis. This breakthrough allowed late 18th-century researchers to finally grasp how plants convert light energy into chemical energy. Ultimately, it is sunlight, rather than soil alone, that powers plant life and oxygen production.
cards.readerQuestion: How did humanity first uncover the secret relationship between plants and sunlight?
cards.keyTakeaway: Sunlight, not just soil, powers plant life and oxygen.

cards.title: The Chemical Blueprint of Light
cards.summary: By formulating the general photosynthetic equation, Cornelis van Niel explained how light-dependent and light-independent stages convert photons into chemical energy. Driven to map the exact chemical pathways of light absorption, researchers soon isolated the specific pigments and equations that power carbon fixation. This process can be oxygenic, which releases oxygen, or anoxygenic, relying instead on alternative electron donors like hydrogen sulfide or arsenite. Scientists eventually mapped both the light-dependent and light-independent reactions, revealing how chlorophyll selectively absorbs red and blue wavelengths while reflecting green. In plants, these chlorophyll pigments absorb only the red and blue spectra, meaning the green hue we perceive is actually reflected light. Ultimately, chlorophyll absorbs these red and blue wavelengths to drive chemical synthesis.
cards.readerQuestion: What chemical equations and light spectra govern the conversion of light into energy?
cards.keyTakeaway: Chlorophyll absorbs red and blue light to power chemical synthesis.

cards.title: Shadows of the Sun
cards.summary: Archaea generate vital ATP using retinal, the very same light-sensitive pigment found in animal eyes. Driven by evolutionary pressures, these diverse organisms developed alternative pigments to capture energy in extreme environments. At least three known organisms rely on shortwave infrared or far-red radiation to power their light-dependent reactions. By using non-visible light spectra and internal gas recycling, life found a way to thrive through these unique adaptations. Sciophytes, for instance, produce such low oxygen levels during photosynthesis that they consume every bit of it themselves. Ultimately, these alternative pigments and extreme adaptations reveal the diverse evolutionary pathways of solar energy capture.
cards.readerQuestion: What biological evidence reveals the diverse evolutionary paths of solar-powered life?
cards.keyTakeaway: Alternative pigments and extreme adaptations reveal diverse evolutionary solar pathways.

provenance.sentence: Archaea generate vital ATP using retinal, the very same light-sensitive pigment found in animal eyes.
provenance.fact: Some archaea perform a type of photosynthesis using retinal, the same pigment used for vision in animals, to generate a proton gradient and synthesize ATP.

provenance.fact: Evolutionary pressures drove diverse organisms to develop alternative pigments and metabolic adaptations to capture energy in extreme or low-light environments.

provenance.fact: While most photosynthetic organisms rely on visible light, at least three known organisms can use shortwave infrared or far-red radiation for their light-dependent reactions.

provenance.sentence: By using non-visible light spectra and internal gas recycling, life found a way to thrive through these unique adaptations.
provenance.fact: These adaptations allowed life to thrive using non-visible light spectra and internal gas recycling, bypassing the traditional limits of oxygenic photosynthesis.

provenance.sentence: Ultimately, these alternative pigments and extreme adaptations reveal the diverse evolutionary pathways of solar energy capture.
provenance.fact: Alternative pigments and extreme adaptations reveal diverse evolutionary solar pathways.

cards.title: The Sun's Living Engine
cards.summary: Globally, photosynthetic organisms capture approximately 130 terawatts of solar energy, a figure that dwarfs human power consumption eight times over. This immense process begins the moment plant pigments absorb solar photons to initiate chemical conversion. Through this systems, these organisms transform up to 115 billion metric tons of carbon into biomass annually. This massive biological conversion drives the planetary carbon cycle, directly opposing cellular respiration. Ultimately, the process converts light energy into chemical energy stored in organic compounds like carbohydrates. By converting sunlight into massive biomass, photosynthesis fuels global life.
cards.readerQuestion: How does the conversion of sunlight shape the energy budget and biomass of our planet?
cards.keyTakeaway: Photosynthesis fuels global life by converting sunlight into massive biomass.

namedEntities.name: Jan Ingenhousz
namedEntities.description: Dutch physiologist who discovered photosynthesis in 1779.

namedEntities.name: Cornelis van Niel
namedEntities.description: Microbiologist who proposed the first generalized chemical equation for photosynthesis.

namedEntities.name: Halobacterium
namedEntities.description: A genus of Archaea that performs a non-carbon-fixing type of anoxygenic photosynthesis using retinal.

namedEntities.name: Cyanobacteria
namedEntities.description: A phylum of gram-negative bacteria that obtain their energy through oxygenic photosynthesis.

namedEntities.name: Calvin cycle
namedEntities.description: A metabolic pathway of light-independent reactions where carbon dioxide is fixed into carbohydrates.

timeline.headline: Earliest Archaeal Photosynthesis
timeline.description: Retinal-based, non-carbon-fixing anoxygenic photosynthesis potentially evolves in archaea like Halobacterium, preceding cyanobacteria.

timeline.headline: Anoxygenic Photosynthesis Emerges
timeline.description: First photosynthetic organisms evolve using reducing agents like hydrogen or hydrogen sulfide instead of water.

timeline.headline: Sulfur-Releasing Photosynthesis Dominates
timeline.description: Purple bacteria splitting hydrogen sulfide dominate the euxinic Canfield oceans, releasing sulfur instead of oxygen.

timeline.headline: Cyanobacteria Oxygenate Earth
timeline.description: Cyanobacteria develop oxygenic photosynthesis, releasing excess oxygen that drives the Great Oxidation Event.

timeline.headline: Discovery of Photosynthesis
timeline.description: Jan Ingenhousz discovers photosynthesis by demonstrating that plants require sunlight to perform the process.

timeline.headline: General Equation Proposed
timeline.description: Cornelis van Niel proposes the generalized chemical equation for photosynthesis using an electron donor.
